data.title: Graham says he's moving 'expeditiously' on ICE, Iran funding under reconciliation
data.description:
The Senate Budget Committee is moving “expeditiously” to draft a second budget reconciliation bill to fund Immigration and Customs Enforcement’s (ICE) removal operations, enact elements of voting reform legislation and fund the war in Iran, according to a statement by Chairman Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.).

Graham says he has the greenlight from President Trump and Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) to begin working on a reconciliation package, which Republicans could pass through the Senate with a simple-majority vote, avoiding a Democratic filibuster.

“After consulting with President Trump and his team and Leader Thune, the Senate Budget Committee will expeditiously move toward creating a second budget reconciliation bill,” Graham said in a statement.

“The purpose of the second reconciliation bill is to make sure there is adequate funding to secure our homeland and to support our men and women in the military who are fighting so bravely,” he said.

Graham said a second reconciliation bill could also be used to implement elements of the SAVE America Act, a voting reform bill that is now on the Senate floor but faces a Democratic filibuster.

“I also think we have many opportunities to improve voter integrity through reconciliation. President Trump and Leader Thune are right to push for a second reconciliation bill to address the threats we face and keep our elections secure and fair,” he said.

Graham and Senate Homeland Security Appropriations Subcommittee Chair Katie Britt (R-Ala.) presented the plan to remove ICE’s Emergency Removal Operations (ERO) funding from the Department of Homeland Security appropriations bill to help its passage through the Senate during a two-hour meeting at the White House Monday.

They pitched the idea of moving more money for immigration enforcement and also elements of the SAVE America Act through a budget reconciliation package later this year.

Sens. Steve Daines (R-Mont.) and Bernie Morena (R-Ohio) also attended the meeting at the White House.

But the plan to split up the Homeland Security appropriations bill and rely on a budget reconciliation bill to enact parts of the SAVE America Act is getting strong pushback from Republicans.

Sen. Rick Scott (R-Fla.), one of Trump’s strongest allies on Capitol Hill, called the plan to enact Trump’s agenda through another reconciliation bill is a “pipe dream.”

“This idea that they’ll get funded through a reconciliation package is a pipe dream. We’re not going to get a reconciliation package done,” Scott told CNBC’s Joe Kernen in an interview.

He said that a reconciliation bill could only be used to pass mandatory spending, not the discretionary spending that is typically used to fund the Homeland Security Department through the regular appropriations process.

Sen. Mike Lee (R-Utah), a leading proponent of the SAVE America Act, said passing it through the reconciliation process would be “impossible” and likely require major revisions to the bill.

“It’s hard to imagine how the SAVE America Act could be passed through reconciliation. And by ‘hard’ I mean ‘essentially impossible,’” Lee said in a post on X.

Passing a reconciliation package through the Senate is an arduous process. It would require the Senate and House first pass a concurrent budget resolution through both chambers, which would require Senate Republicans to slog through an all-night vote-a-rama.

Then the Senate and House would have to pass a separate reconciliation bill, which would require another late-night vote-a-rama in the Senate, giving Democrats many opportunity to force Republicans to take votes on tough amendments that could be used as the basis for campaign ads in the fall.

Republicans spent months working on the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, which eventually passed through the budget reconciliation process.

The bill extended Trump’s 2017 tax cuts and implemented new tax relief as well as cuts to Medicaid and the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP).

data.title: Watch live: House Democrats give remarks amid DHS funding battle, Iran turmoil
data.description:
House Democratic Caucus Chair Pete Aguilar (D-Calif.) and other Dem leaders will speak with the media Wednesday morning as the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) shutdown drags on, despite pressure on both sides to come to a deal to reopen the department.

Democrats have largely held firm on their demands for reforms to the Trump administration’s immigration enforcement tactics — but long lines at airport security checkpoints and more than 400 resignations within the Transportation Security Administration have upped the ante after funding lapsed for the DHS-level agencies over a month ago.

Many have also questioned President Trump’s decision to launch joint strikes with Israel against Iran late last month and efforts to block the military operation have failed. Retaliatory strikes by Iranian forces on U.S. military bases in neighboring Gulf countries and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz have driven up energy prices globally.

The U.S. has offered a 15-point peace plan to end the Middle East conflict, though the Islamic Republic has dismissed the initial proposal.

The press conference is scheduled to begin at 11:45 a.m. EDT.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: Bolton on Trump claiming Iran regime change success: 'Ideology remains the same'
data.description:
Former national security adviser John Bolton cast doubt on President Trump’s claims of successful regime change in Iran this week. 

The president said during an Oval Office press conference on Tuesday that the Iranian “leaders are all very different than the ones that we started off with that created all those problems.”

Bolton told CNN’s Anderson Cooper later on Tuesday that the extent of turnover in Iranian leadership does “absolutely not” amount to what the president has stated. 

“The faces may change, but the ideology remains the same,” he said. “The regime will change when the ayatollahs and the revolutionary guard are gone, and we’re not at that point yet, quite obviously. But it’s another way for him to say that we have won the objective and therefore are victorious and can leave.”

Joint U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran have killed Iranian Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, Iranian Supreme National Security Council Secretary Ali Larijani, Iranian Intelligence Minister Esmail Khatib and the head of the paramilitary Revolutionary Guard’s Basij force, Gen. Gholam Reza Soleimani. 

Bolton was national security adviser during Trump’s first term. He also served as the U.S. ambassador to the United Nations during George W. Bush’s presidency.

The White House sent over a 15-point ceasefire proposal to Tehran this week, which Iranian officials rejected, launching additional strikes in the Gulf on Tuesday night. 

This negotiation effort follows Trump’s threat that he would attack Iran’s power plants unless the country reopened the Strait of Hormuz. 

The president later rescinded his ultimatum, citing “good and productive” peace talks. The Strait of Hormuz, a major oil trading corridor, has been effectively closed due to Iranian counterstrikes on U.S. bases and energy infrastructure in the region. 

The Pentagon is preparing to deploy around 2,000 U.S. troops from the Army’s 82nd Airborne Division to the Middle East as part of the ongoing military operations against Iran. 

Around 50,000 U.S. servicemembers are already in the U.S. Central Command region, and 290 have been wounded since the beginning of the conflict in late February. 

Bolton told CNN on Tuesday evening that the Trump administration’s moves during these military operations against Iran signify a “lack of strategic thinking.”

“It could be that the delay in negotiations or the delay in the deadline for further strikes on energy targets inside Iran is to give time for whatever forces need to be assembled, for whatever objective they’re looking at taking on, and that would make the delay more understandable,” Bolton said. “But I have a more general sense that things are being done on a day-by-day basis, that new plans are evolving, and it reflects a lack of strategic thinking before the attack began.”

data.title: Savannah Guthrie says she's in 'agony' over mother's disappearance
data.description:
NBC host Savannah Guthrie returned to the set of the “Today” show for her first interview since her 84-year-old mother was reported missing earlier this year, describing the “agony” her family has felt in the weeks since Nancy Guthrie disappeared.

Guthrie sat down with her co-host, Hoda Kotb, for an emotional conversation that is set to air in full in two parts on Thursday and Friday. In a preview clip shared Wednesday morning, Guthrie pleaded with the public for information on her mother’s whereabouts.

“Someone needs to do the right thing,” she said, while teary-eyed. “We are in agony. It is unbearable.”

“And to think of what she went through. I wake up every night in the middle of the night, every night. And in the darkness, I imagine her terror. And it is unthinkable, but those thoughts demand to be thought,” Guthrie continued. “And I will not hide my face. But she needs to come home now.”

Nancy Guthrie was reported missing from her Tucson, Ariz., home on Feb. 1, after she failed to show up for church. She was last seen being dropped off at home by a family member around 9 p.m. on Jan. 31.

The FBI and Pima County Sheriff’s Department have been sifting through tens of thousands of tips and searching the region around her home, but are still awaiting a breakthrough in the investigation, now in its 8th week.

Authorities believe that Guthrie was abducted as doorbell footage released by the FBI shows an armed, masked individual wearing gloves and a backpack approaching Guthrie’s home and tampering with the camera before her disappearance.

The suspect — described as a roughly 5-foot-9 to 5-foot-10-inch-tall male with an average build — has not yet been publicly identified.

The family is offering a reward of up to $1 million for information leading to their mother’s recovery.

“We continue to believe it is Tucsonans, and the greater southern Arizona community, that hold the key to finding resolution in this case. Someone knows something,” the family said in a March 23 statement shared with KVOA in Tucson. “It’s possible a member of this community has information that they do not even realize is significant.”

Neighbors told NBC News last week that investigators have been to their homes to ask about specific dates, including the weekend Guthrie disappeared. Authorities have also reportedly visited gun shops in the area seeking information about recent purchases.

“There is a desperation and also a steeliness about Savannah,” Kotb told her colleagues on Wednesday morning. “I mean, she’s hoping that somebody, whoever this person is, will see something and say something.”

data.title: Price tags for patients: I'm still fighting for honest health care transparency
data.description:
One of my top priorities during my final years in Congress was bipartisan health care price-transparency legislation to empower patients and create incentives to reduce outrageous costs. Our bill came close but ultimately didn’t make it all the way through the legislative process.

I no longer serve in Congress, yet I believe more than ever that maximizing price transparency is the key to fixing American health care. In fact, leaving the Capitol has only intensified my passion for accomplishing this foundational reform. That’s why I’m asking my former colleagues to come together to pass the Patients Deserve Price Tags Act and reverse the runaway costs of care and coverage.

My husband and I recently spent time in the emergency room seeking necessary care for him. In moments like these, you don’t think about policy debates or legislation — you think about getting care as quickly as possible. We were grateful to the doctors and nurses who helped him and thankful that he recovered well.

But these situations are made more stressful as we, like most American families, fly blind on cost. Without upfront prices, it is very difficult to navigate the U.S. health care system and avoid huge medical bills.

Fortunately, my family receives health coverage through Tricare, thanks to my husband’s service in the Navy. That shields us from the costs that many other families face. For them, a single emergency room visit can mean devastating out-of-pocket costs and ever-rising annual premiums.

By requiring upfront prices throughout the health care system, the Patients Deserve Price Tags Act protects patients and their families from overcharges and helps them choose the best care at the best value. Price transparency would be a victory for everyone who has struggled to understand health care and prescription drug costs.

Price-transparency allows patients to spot wide price variations for the same care — even at the same hospital. For example, a new study by 3 Axis Advisors shows the price of the same chemotherapy can range between $12,000 to $43,000, and the same immunotherapy varies between $17,000 to $67,000.

This legislation can also restore employer-sponsored coverage, once a reliable pillar of American health care. According to the health policy nonprofit KFF, the average annual employer-sponsored family health premium is now $27,000, not including co-pays and deductibles. Escalating premium costs are reducing workers’ take-home pay and wage increases, and forcing more and more small businesses to drop coverage altogether.

The Patients Deserve Price Tags Act finally would finally give employers access to their claims data so they can spot hospital price gouging, insurance overbilling, and kickbacks that raise plan costs. With this information, they can design affordable health plans and share savings with their employees through lower premiums and higher wages. It’s no surprise 40 economists from all backgrounds say price transparency can save the American economy $1 trillion per year.

The legislation is also vital to families like mine with government insurance plans, including Tricare, Medicaid and Medicare. Such coverage often means long waits, difficulty securing appointments and limited provider choices. In those moments, many of us start wondering whether it would make more sense to just pay cash and get care sooner. Transparent pricing would let us weigh the trade-off between waiting for insurance and paying cash now, reducing wait times for others in the process.

Health care price transparency is an issue that should unite Congress. It is supported by a bipartisan supermajority of 90 percent of Americans because it isn’t left versus right — it is a matter of fairness and affordability. It is a fight worth continuing, and I intend to see it through.

Cathy McMorris Rodgers is the former U.S. representative for Washington’s 5th Congressional District.

data.title: Watch live: Melania Trump hosts first spouses at White House roundtable
data.description:
First lady Melania Trump will host a group of first spouses from more than 40 countries at the White House on Wednesday morning.

The roundtable event is part of the inaugural two-day global summit for Trump’s Fostering the Future Together initiative. The first lady, in remarks from the State Department on Tuesday, called for allies to work together to improve access to education and technology for children around the world.

According to the guidance, the Wednesday session will focus on how other nations are “supporting children’s well-being in the digital age.”

The event is scheduled to begin at 11 a.m. EDT.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: Supreme Court rules for Cox in Sony copyright fight over music downloads
data.description:
The Supreme Court unanimously ruled Wednesday that Sony cannot hold Cox Communications liable for not doing enough to disconnect customers who illegally downloaded copyrighted music, a battle that has sent ripples through the music and telecommunications industries.  

Justice Clarence Thomas, writing for the majority, said a lower court got it wrong in putting Cox on the hook for damages merely because it refused to disconnect customers it allegedly knew were repeat infringers.

Instead, the justices ruled that music labels can only sue when an internet service provider actually intended for its product to be used for copyright infringement, or if they tailor the product for infringers’ illegal activity.

“Under our precedents, a company is not liable as a copyright infringer for merely providing a service to the general public with knowledge that it will be used by some to infringe copyrights,” Thomas wrote. 

Sony and other music labels sued Cox Communications in 2018 after alerting the company about repeat infringers. Sony says Cox didn’t cut off those customers. 

The case has jolted the music and telecommunications industries ever since Sony won a $1 billion judgment against Cox at trial. The battle has also attracted interest from First Amendment and civil rights groups, who believe it poses broader free speech implications for bookstores to social media platforms. 

The lawsuit is aimed at 57,000 Cox customer accounts who were found to infringe over 10,000 copyrighted works. 

The Supreme Court got involved after the U.S. Court of Appeals for the 4th Circuit wiped the 10-figure award in 2024.  

Still, that wasn’t a complete win for Cox. The 4th Circuit only rejected one of Sony’s two legal theories. 

The appeals court ruled Cox remained on the hook for “contributory” copyright infringement, saying the company must pay damages for any specific users it did not terminate but knew were infringing. 

Cox argued that decision is wrong and poses stark consequences. The company stressed it doesn’t profit from the illegal activity and Cox doesn’t encourage infringement — Cox’s terms of service explicitly prohibit it. 

The company got the backing of the Trump administration, which warned that finding the Cox liable would threaten universal internet access. 

“The decision below does not distinguish between a single user’s account, a family account with multiple members, a university account shared by thousands of students, or a regional ISP that uses Cox’s services to provide internet access to tens of thousands on a single account,” the administration wrote in court filings.

data.title: Jeffries warns Republicans on redistricting after Democrat flips Mar-a-Lago seat
data.description:
House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) warned Republicans that their redistricting efforts could end up working against them after a Democrat won a special election in Florida on Tuesday. 

“Democrats FLIPPED a state House seat in Palm Beach that Trump won by 11 points in 2024. Mar-a-Lago will now be represented by Emily Gregory, a strong Democratic voice,” Jeffries wrote in a post on X on Tuesday.

“We will crush House Republicans in November if DeSantis tries to gerrymander the Florida congressional map,” he added.

Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis (R) has called for a spring special legislative session focused on redistricting in the Sunshine State. The session is set to convene next month. 

Some have already challenged the effort, alleging DeSantis doesn’t have the authority to order the state House to redraw maps.

Republicans currently have a 20-8 advantage in the House and are looking to add as many as five more seats for the midterms through redistricting.   

Jeffries says Gregory’s win is a sign of broader wave rising against the GOP. 

Gregory, a health fitness small business owner, defeated President Trump-backed Republican Jon Maples by 2.2 percentage points, according to Decision Desk HQ’s initial count. The House district includes part of Palm Beach County and the president’s Mar-a-Lago resort.  

Her win follows an upset in a Texas state senate race earlier this year, when Democrat Taylor Rehmet defeated Republican Leigh Wambsganss in a district favored by Trump. 

The Lone Star State kicked off a nationwide redistricting battle last year after it redrew congressional maps in favor of Republicans, forgoing the traditional once-a-decade timeline that’s based on the U.S. Census. 

Lawmakers in California, New York, Maryland and other states quickly followed suit by reworking their congressional maps.

data.title: Live updates: DHS funding deal eludes lawmakers; Iran reportedly rejects Trump's ceasefire plan
data.description:
Lawmakers are scrambling to end the partial government shutdown ahead of a planned two-week recess as both Democrats and Republicans find fault with a compromise plan.

President Trump indicated earlier this week he was open to funding the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) without new money for federal immigration enforcement — an idea that GOP leaders have rejected for weeks.

A ceasefire plan to end the war against Iran has been rejected by Tehran. The U.S. sent a 15-point proposal to Iran through intermediaries, Pakistani officials unveiled Wednesday, though the Islamic Republic seemingly dismissed the terms, according to The Associated Press.

In Grapevine, Texas, the annual Conservative Political Action Conference gets underway. Here’s what to watch for.

White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt will brief reporters at 1 p.m. And Trump on Wednesday evening will deliver remarks at the annual National Republican Congressional Committee dinner in Washington, D.C.

Follow live below.

data.title: OpenAI shutting down video generator Sora
data.description:
OpenAI announced on Tuesday it will shut down its AI-powered video generator app Sora, but did not provide further details on the decision. 

“We’re saying goodbye to Sora. To everyone who created with Sora shared it, and built community around it: thank you,” the company wrote in post Tuesday. “What you made with Sora mattered, and we know this news is disappointing.”

”We’ll share more soon, including timelines for the app and API and details on preserving your work,” the X post continued, signed by “The Sora Team.” 

The app was released in late 2024, gaining quick attention for its ability to produce vivid and realistic video clips with text prompts. The videos were often humorous in nature, often going viral on major social media platforms.  

But various advocacy groups and experts also raised concerns it could lead to an increase in nonconsensual images and realistic deepfakes. These concerns were amplified in September, when OpenAI released the second-generation Sora model, which created even more realistic video clips. 

In shutting down the app, OpenAI appears to be ending its partnership with Disney, which was announced just three months ago. The three-year licensing agreement, which included a $1 billion equity investment into OpenAI from Disney, gave Sora access to more than 200 Disney, Marvel, Pixar and Star Wars characters. 

The Hill reached out to Disney for comment. 

OpenAI indicated in recent months that the heavy use of Sora was straining the company’s servers and the head of Sora said last November that the firm’s graphics processing units (GPUs) “are melting.” The company limited the number of video generations as a result. 

It comes ahead of a possible initial public offering for OpenAI later this year and follows various business changes at the company, the Wall Street Journal first reported.
